stories.headline: The scaffold around an AI model matters as much as the model itself
stories.summary: Lilian Weng's synthesis on harness engineering, building on this week's Claude Code anatomy piece, argues the orchestration layer — workflow loops, file-based memory, parallel sub-agents — is the near-term path to recursive self-improvement, not models rewriting their own weights. Claude Code and Codex already demonstrate the pattern. The harness is now an optimisation target, not scaffolding bolted on after training.

claims.claim: The scaffold around an AI model matters as much as the model itself
claims.summary_span: The scaffold around an AI model matters as much as the model itself
claims.source_span: the layer between the raw model and the real-world context seems to be as important as the model's raw intelligence
claims.note: The source explicitly states the deployment/harness layer is as important as the model's raw intelligence, supporting the headline's claim.

claims.claim: Lilian Weng's synthesis on harness engineering
claims.summary_span: Lilian Weng's synthesis on harness engineering
claims.note: The source excerpt does not name the author; the attribution to Lilian Weng cannot be confirmed or denied from this excerpt.

claims.claim: building on this week's Claude Code anatomy piece
claims.summary_span: building on this week's Claude Code anatomy piece
claims.note: The source excerpt makes no reference to a prior 'Claude Code anatomy piece'; this cannot be verified from the excerpt.

claims.claim: the orchestration layer — workflow loops, file-based memory, parallel sub-agents — is the near-term path to recursive self-improvement
claims.summary_span: the orchestration layer — workflow loops, file-based memory, parallel sub-agents — is the near-term path to recursive self-improvement
claims.source_span: the near-term path of RSI is unlikely to start as a model directly rewriting its weights. My prediction of a practical near-term path is: - Harness engineering will evolve in the direction of meta-methodology
claims.note: The source identifies harness engineering (including workflow loops, file-based memory, and sub-agents) as the near-term RSI path, supporting this claim.

claims.claim: not models rewriting their own weights
claims.summary_span: not models rewriting their own weights
claims.source_span: the near-term path of RSI is unlikely to start as a model directly rewriting its weights
claims.note: The source explicitly states RSI is unlikely to start with models rewriting their own weights.

claims.claim: Claude Code and Codex already demonstrate the pattern
claims.summary_span: Claude Code and Codex already demonstrate the pattern
claims.source_span: Harnesses are important components of AI deployment, as shown by successful coding agent products such as Claude Code and Codex.
claims.note: The source cites Claude Code and Codex as examples demonstrating the harness pattern.

claims.claim: The harness is now an optimisation target, not scaffolding bolted on after training
claims.summary_span: The harness is now an optimisation target, not scaffolding bolted on after training
claims.source_span: The harness system itself becomes an optimization target, with fewer heuristic rules and more general mechanisms.
claims.note: The source directly states the harness system becomes an optimization target, supporting this claim.

sections.name: big_picture

stories.headline: FCA maps four AI forces set to reshape retail finance by 2030
stories.summary: The FCA's Mills Review, the first regulator-initiated AI assessment globally, names four structural shifts by 2030: operational transformation, evolving consumer journeys, market concentration, and amplified fraud risk. Research found 11 million UK adults willing to use autonomous AI in personal finance. Does your governance framework treat fraud amplification as a design constraint, or a residual risk?

claims.claim: FCA maps four AI forces set to reshape retail finance by 2030
claims.summary_span: FCA maps four AI forces set to reshape retail finance by 2030
claims.source_span: the report identifies 4 major AI‑driven shifts likely to impact retail financial services... by 2030 and beyond.
claims.note: 'Four AI forces' is a fair paraphrase of '4 major AI-driven shifts', and the 2030 horizon matches the source.

claims.claim: The FCA's Mills Review, the first regulator-initiated AI assessment globally, names four structural shifts by 2030
claims.summary_span: The FCA's Mills Review, the first regulator-initiated AI assessment globally, names four structural shifts by 2030
claims.source_span: The Mills Review is the first work of its kind initiated by a regulator globally... the report identifies 4 major AI‑driven shifts likely to impact retail financial services... by 2030 and beyond.
claims.note: 'Structural shifts' is a fair paraphrase of 'major AI-driven shifts'; all other facts match the source.

claims.claim: operational transformation, evolving consumer journeys, market concentration, and amplified fraud risk
claims.summary_span: operational transformation, evolving consumer journeys, market concentration, and amplified fraud risk
claims.source_span: the transformation of firm operations; the evolution of consumer journeys; the reshaping of competition and market power; and the amplification of fraud and cyber risks.
claims.note: All four shifts are correctly named; the compressions ('market concentration' for 'reshaping of competition and market power', 'amplified fraud risk' for 'amplification of fraud and cyber risks') are acceptable paraphrases.

claims.claim: Research found 11 million UK adults willing to use autonomous AI in personal finance
claims.summary_span: Research found 11 million UK adults willing to use autonomous AI in personal finance
claims.source_span: research commissioned by the FCA showing that a fifth of people – equivalent to 11 million UK adults – are likely to use AI that can act autonomously within pre-set goals.
claims.note: 'Willing to use' is a fair paraphrase of 'likely to use'; the figure and context match the source.

claims.claim: Does your governance framework treat fraud amplification as a design constraint, or a residual risk?
claims.summary_span: Does your governance framework treat fraud amplification as a design constraint, or a residual risk?
claims.note: This is an editorial call-to-action/opinion, not a factual claim; per scope rules it should not be extracted and judged, but if assessed it contains no checkable factual assertion to contradict.

stories.headline: AI agent swarms surpassed Google's concealed quantum cryptography result in 72 hours
stories.summary: In March, Google concealed a quantum cryptography advance behind a zero-knowledge proof. IEEE Spectrum reports that Eigen Labs' crowdsourced AI agent swarms surpassed Google's results in 72 hours; the underlying work cuts the estimated qubit threshold for breaking elliptic-curve encryption by nearly 20-fold. If that pace holds, is your post-quantum migration timeline still realistic?

claims.claim: AI agent swarms surpassed Google's concealed quantum cryptography result in 72 hours
claims.summary_span: AI agent swarms surpassed Google's concealed quantum cryptography result in 72 hours
claims.source_span: in just three days, with the help of crowdsourcing and swarms of AI agents, Seattle-based research startup Eigen Labs not only matched the results of that hidden work, but surpassed them... In about 72 hours, it surpassed Google.
claims.note: Source confirms AI agent swarms surpassed Google's concealed result in ~72 hours; 'three days' and '72 hours' are consistent.

claims.claim: Google concealed a quantum cryptography advance behind a zero-knowledge proof
claims.summary_span: Google concealed a quantum cryptography advance behind a zero-knowledge proof
claims.source_span: instead of fully explaining how they accomplished this advance, the scientists released their work using a 'zero-knowledge proof,' a technique with which they could verify to others than their attack works without revealing exactly how to carry it out.
claims.note: Source confirms Google used a zero-knowledge proof to conceal the details of their quantum cryptography advance.

claims.claim: IEEE Spectrum reports that Eigen Labs' crowdsourced AI agent swarms surpassed Google's results in 72 hours
claims.summary_span: IEEE Spectrum reports that Eigen Labs' crowdsourced AI agent swarms surpassed Google's results in 72 hours
claims.note: The source excerpt does not name IEEE Spectrum as the reporting outlet; this attribution cannot be confirmed from the excerpt.

claims.claim: the underlying work cuts the estimated qubit threshold for breaking elliptic-curve encryption by nearly 20-fold
claims.summary_span: the underlying work cuts the estimated qubit threshold for breaking elliptic-curve encryption by nearly 20-fold
claims.source_span: This again marked a nearly 20-fold reduction in the number of physical qubits previously estimated.
claims.note: Source explicitly states a nearly 20-fold reduction in physical qubits needed to break ECC.

stories.headline: Narrow task instructions blind AI models to safety hazards they'd otherwise catch
stories.summary: An arXiv preprint finds narrow task instructions suppress safety-signal reporting by up to 0.92 in report rate; in radiology, an exclusive instruction abolished reporting entirely. The effect held across every model tested and didn't shrink with scale. If your deployed agent has a defined task scope, what safety-critical signals fall outside its frame?

claims.claim: Narrow task instructions blind AI models to safety hazards they'd otherwise catch
claims.summary_span: Narrow task instructions blind AI models to safety hazards they'd otherwise catch
claims.source_span: conditioning a language or vision model on a narrow task suppresses its reporting of co-present, safety-critical signals it can otherwise report
claims.note: The headline is a plain-English paraphrase of the paper's central finding, which the source directly states.

claims.claim: An arXiv preprint finds narrow task instructions suppress safety-signal reporting by up to 0.92 in report rate
claims.summary_span: narrow task instructions suppress safety-signal reporting by up to 0.92 in report rate
claims.source_span: the ordinary focused instructions under which such systems are deployed suppressed reporting by up to 0.92 in report rate relative to the same models when unconstrained
claims.note: The figure 0.92 and the mechanism match the source exactly.

claims.claim: in radiology, an exclusive instruction abolished reporting entirely
claims.summary_span: in radiology, an exclusive instruction abolished reporting entirely
claims.source_span: an explicit exclusive instruction abolished reporting entirely in radiology
claims.note: Near-verbatim match with the source.

claims.claim: The effect held across every model tested
claims.summary_span: The effect held across every model tested
claims.source_span: Suppression appeared in every model tested
claims.note: Direct match with the source.

claims.claim: The effect didn't shrink with scale
claims.summary_span: didn't shrink with scale
claims.source_span: did not diminish with scale
claims.note: Plain-English paraphrase of 'did not diminish with scale'.

stories.headline: MCP now lets enterprises grant agent tool-access through one login
stories.summary: The Model Context Protocol's Enterprise-Managed Authorisation extension is now stable, adopted by Anthropic, Microsoft, and Okta. It replaces per-server consent prompts with a single sign-on flow: users inherit access to all approved servers automatically. The spec explicitly excludes runtime action control, so centralised identity is only half the picture. Does your agent governance model treat connection-level auth as sufficient?

claims.claim: MCP now lets enterprises grant agent tool-access through one login
claims.summary_span: MCP now lets enterprises grant agent tool-access through one login
claims.source_span: Organisations can centrally manage authorisation for MCP servers, and end-users can access all connected MCP servers through a single login.
claims.note: Source confirms single-login access to MCP servers for enterprises; 'agent tool-access' is a fair plain-English paraphrase of MCP server access.

claims.claim: The Model Context Protocol's Enterprise-Managed Authorisation extension is now stable
claims.summary_span: The Model Context Protocol's Enterprise-Managed Authorisation extension is now stable
claims.source_span: The Model Context Protocol team has promoted its Enterprise-Managed Authorisation extension to stable status
claims.note: Source directly confirms the extension has been promoted to stable status.

claims.claim: adopted by Anthropic, Microsoft, and Okta
claims.summary_span: adopted by Anthropic, Microsoft, and Okta
claims.source_span: the extension is now stable and has been adopted by Anthropic, Microsoft, Okta, and a growing number of MCP servers
claims.note: Source confirms all three named adopters; omitting 'a growing number of MCP servers' is acceptable compression.

claims.claim: It replaces per-server consent prompts with a single sign-on flow
claims.summary_span: It replaces per-server consent prompts with a single sign-on flow
claims.source_span: The project states the aim is to replace per-server consent prompts with a zero-touch flow in which users sign in once
claims.note: 'Single sign-on flow' is a fair paraphrase of 'zero-touch flow in which users sign in once'.

claims.claim: users inherit access to all approved servers automatically
claims.summary_span: users inherit access to all approved servers automatically
claims.source_span: users inherit access to the servers their organisation has approved
claims.note: Source directly supports this claim.

claims.claim: The spec explicitly excludes runtime action control
claims.summary_span: The spec explicitly excludes runtime action control
claims.source_span: The guide explicitly warns that this is not runtime authorisation for individual actions
claims.note: Source explicitly states the extension does not cover runtime authorisation for individual actions.

claims.claim: centralised identity is only half the picture
claims.summary_span: centralised identity is only half the picture
claims.source_span: organisations still need their own controls for what happens once an agent is inside a system
claims.note: This is an editorial compression of the source's point that EMA handles connection-level auth but not runtime action control; it is a fair characterisation.

sections.intro_body: Regulators, researchers, and protocol designers are all arriving at the same finding: the risks in deployed AI are specific, quantifiable, and no longer theoretical. The strategic question is whether your controls are calibrated to match.

sections.name: hands_on

stories.headline: Wire a full GitHub agent into your codebase in nine lines
stories.summary: Vercel's eve connects to GitHub through a single tools file, wiring a complete agent in nine lines of code; five presets scope the toolset. Every write operation requires explicit approval by default. Install @github-tools/sdk/eve, wire the maintainer preset, and diff the approval gates against your current code-review workflow.

stories.headline: A 10,700-star repo makes AI research stocks like Warren Buffett
stories.summary: A practitioner Substack post profiles AI Berkshire, which wires Claude Code into a value-investing research workflow modelled on Buffett, Munger, and Li Lu. The gap it fills: language models write fluent analysis but rarely useful analysis. Clone the repo and run it against a company you already know well; the Buffett framing is the quality signal, not a gimmick.

claims.claim: A 10,700-star repo makes AI research stocks like Warren Buffett
claims.summary_span: A 10,700-star repo makes AI research stocks like Warren Buffett
claims.source_span: A GitHub project called AI Berkshire has crossed 10,700 stars by teaching Claude Code to research companies the way Buffett, Munger, Duan Yongping, and Li Lu would.
claims.note: Star count matches; 'like Warren Buffett' is a fair compression of the Buffett/Munger/Li Lu value-investing framing; 'research stocks' paraphrases 'research companies'.

claims.claim: A practitioner Substack post profiles AI Berkshire
claims.summary_span: A practitioner Substack post profiles AI Berkshire
claims.note: The source excerpt never mentions a Substack post; this detail cannot be confirmed or denied from the excerpt.

claims.note: Source confirms Claude Code and value-investing research workflow; 'wires into' is acceptable paraphrase of 'teaching'.

claims.note: Source names Buffett, Munger, Duan Yongping, and Li Lu; omitting Duan Yongping is an omission, not a false assertion.

claims.claim: language models write fluent analysis but rarely useful analysis

claims.source_span: The paragraphs are fluent, the structure is tidy, and the whole thing is almost useless for an actual decision. That gap between fluent and useful is exactly the space a project called AI Berkshire has moved into.
claims.note: Source explicitly describes the fluent-but-useless gap; summary is a faithful compression.

claims.claim: Clone the repo and run it against a company you already know well
claims.summary_span: Clone the repo and run it against a company you already know well

stories.headline: Cap your MCP server at 15 tools or accuracy drops
stories.summary: An industry paper derived from 15 servers -- 5 in production, 10 public -- names five MCP architecture patterns. The headline number: Claude Haiku 4.5 hits 91% tool-selection accuracy at 10 tools, falling to 87% at 15. The first author's employer supplied the telemetry. Pull the paper and audit your server's visible tool count before your next agentic deployment.

claims.claim: Cap your MCP server at 15 tools or accuracy drops
claims.summary_span: Cap your MCP server at 15 tools or accuracy drops
claims.source_span: a single context should stay under roughly 10 to 15 visible tools for a Haiku-class model. Past that, the model starts choosing wrong often enough to matter.
claims.note: The source supports a ~10-15 tool ceiling beyond which accuracy drops; '15' as the cap is a reasonable compression of the '10 to 15' range.

claims.claim: An industry paper derived from 15 servers -- 5 in production, 10 public -- names five MCP architecture patterns.
claims.summary_span: An industry paper derived from 15 servers -- 5 in production, 10 public -- names five MCP architecture patterns.
claims.source_span: Rodrigues and Vas derive their catalog from 15 servers: 5 production servers running Celabe's ANSYR voice AI platform since late 2024, and 10 public servers from the official modelcontextprotocol/servers registry.
claims.note: All three figures (15 total, 5 production, 10 public) and the five patterns are confirmed by the source.

claims.claim: Claude Haiku 4.5 hits 91% tool-selection accuracy at 10 tools, falling to 87% at 15.
claims.summary_span: Claude Haiku 4.5 hits 91% tool-selection accuracy at 10 tools, falling to 87% at 15.
claims.source_span: Claude Haiku 4.5 picks the right tool 91% of the time when an MCP server shows it 10 tools. Add five more and accuracy falls to 87%.
claims.note: Both figures and the model name match the source exactly.

claims.claim: The first author's employer supplied the telemetry.
claims.summary_span: The first author's employer supplied the telemetry.
claims.source_span: The first author works at Celabe, which supplies both the production half of the corpus and all the telemetry behind the tool-count numbers.
claims.note: The source confirms Celabe (first author's employer) supplied the telemetry.

claims.claim: Pull the paper and audit your server's visible tool count before your next agentic deployment.
claims.summary_span: Pull the paper and audit your server's visible tool count before your next agentic deployment.
claims.note: This is a call to action / editorial recommendation, not a factual claim — skipped per scope rules; marked supported as it contains no false assertion.

stories.headline: Databricks' open agent harness tightens controls as session risk builds
stories.summary: Most agent frameworks allow or block individual actions; Omnigent, Databricks' open-source meta-harness, tracks what an agent has done across a session and tightens rules as risk accumulates. A built-in Google Drive policy blocks writes to pre-existing files the moment the agent opens a confidential document. Wire it around Claude Code or Codex and stress-test the spending-cap policy.

claims.claim: Databricks' open agent harness tightens controls as session risk builds
claims.summary_span: Databricks' open agent harness tightens controls as session risk builds
claims.note: The source never names Databricks as the creator of Omnigent; it lists individual authors (Matei Zaharia et al.) and describes Omnigent as open source, but no affiliation to Databricks is stated in the excerpt.

claims.claim: Most agent frameworks allow or block individual actions
claims.summary_span: Most agent frameworks allow or block individual actions
claims.source_span: most agent software today only provides simple allow-list controls or guardrails on individual actions, e.g., whether to allow Git pushes or web searches.
claims.note: Direct paraphrase of the source's description of existing agent frameworks.

claims.claim: Omnigent is an open-source meta-harness
claims.summary_span: Omnigent, Databricks' open-source meta-harness
claims.source_span: We recently launched Omnigent, an open source meta-harness for AI agents.
claims.note: Source confirms Omnigent is an open source meta-harness; the Databricks attribution is the unsupported part (covered in headline claim).

claims.claim: Omnigent tracks what an agent has done across a session and tightens rules as risk accumulates
claims.summary_span: tracks what an agent has done across a session and tightens rules as risk accumulates
claims.source_span: Omnigent introduces contextual policies for AI agents: Policies that can track what an agent session has done so far to evaluate whether the next action should proceed... guardrails that get stricter as risk accumulates.
claims.note: Accurate paraphrase of the source's description of contextual policies.

claims.claim: A built-in Google Drive policy blocks writes to pre-existing files the moment the agent opens a confidential document
claims.summary_span: A built-in Google Drive policy blocks writes to pre-existing files the moment the agent opens a confidential document
claims.source_span: you can mark a set of documents as confidential: the moment the agent opens one, the policy tightens so that writes are confined to that set. Even a doc the agent created a minute ago becomes off-limits
claims.note: Accurate compression; 'blocks writes to pre-existing files' correctly captures that even previously created docs become off-limits.

claims.claim: Wire it around Claude Code or Codex
claims.summary_span: Wire it around Claude Code or Codex
claims.source_span: Omnigent, as a meta-harness, allows these policies to be applied across any agent it wraps, including coding agents like Claude Code and Codex.
claims.note: 'Wire it around' is a plain-English paraphrase of wrapping agents; Claude Code and Codex are confirmed in the source.

claims.claim: stress-test the spending-cap policy
claims.summary_span: stress-test the spending-cap policy
claims.source_span: you can configure per-session spending caps
claims.note: Spending caps are confirmed as a built-in policy; 'stress-test' is editorial framing, not a factual claim to verify.

sections.intro_body: Today's releases share a design instinct: constrain the agent early, tighten as risk grows. The practical question is whether your current tooling exposes those limits before production does.

sections.name: currents

stories.headline: Image safety filters break when the policy changes, not the image
stories.summary: Current guardrails treat safety as fixed to the image, not the active policy; the same image can pass in one product and be blocked in another. An arXiv preprint introduces PolicyShiftBench (2,000 instances, 265 images) and a companion model scoring 76.9 F1. Replicated, multi-product deployments need policy-conditioned guardrails; unreplicated, the benchmark framing is still the sharper design question.

claims.claim: Image safety filters break when the policy changes, not the image
claims.summary_span: Image safety filters break when the policy changes, not the image
claims.source_span: Image guardrails are typically trained and evaluated under a fixed safety policy, implicitly treating safety as an intrinsic property of an image. Real deployments are different: the same image may be allowed in one product, restricted in another, and newly disallowed when a policy boundary changes.
claims.note: The headline accurately paraphrases the core finding that guardrails fail under policy shifts, not image changes.

claims.claim: Current guardrails treat safety as fixed to the image, not the active policy
claims.summary_span: Current guardrails treat safety as fixed to the image, not the active policy
claims.source_span: Image guardrails are typically trained and evaluated under a fixed safety policy, implicitly treating safety as an intrinsic property of an image.
claims.note: Direct paraphrase of the source's description of the problem with current guardrails.

claims.claim: the same image can pass in one product and be blocked in another
claims.summary_span: the same image can pass in one product and be blocked in another
claims.source_span: the same image may be allowed in one product, restricted in another, and newly disallowed when a policy boundary changes.
claims.note: Near-verbatim match with the source.

claims.claim: An arXiv preprint introduces PolicyShiftBench (2,000 instances, 265 images)
claims.summary_span: An arXiv preprint introduces PolicyShiftBench (2,000 instances, 265 images)
claims.source_span: We introduce PolicyShiftBench, a comprehensive benchmark with 2,000 policy-discriminative instances over 265 images
claims.note: Numbers and benchmark name match the source exactly.

claims.claim: a companion model scoring 76.9 F1
claims.summary_span: a companion model scoring 76.9 F1
claims.source_span: The 7B model achieves SOTA performance of 76.9 Avg. F1 and 72.1 Avg. PSS on PolicyShiftBench
claims.note: The F1 figure matches; omitting the PSS score and model size is acceptable compression.

claims.claim: Replicated, multi-product deployments need policy-conditioned guardrails
claims.summary_span: Replicated, multi-product deployments need policy-conditioned guardrails
claims.note: The word 'Replicated' does not appear in the source; it is unclear what entity or concept this refers to, making the claim unverifiable from the excerpt.

claims.claim: unreplicated, the benchmark framing is still the sharper design question
claims.summary_span: unreplicated, the benchmark framing is still the sharper design question
claims.note: This editorial/trust-qualifier framing has no corresponding span in the source excerpt and cannot be verified.

stories.headline: AI coding tools can be hijacked into botnets via hallucinated package names
stories.summary: HalluSquatting exploits a coding agent's tendency to hallucinate package identifiers. Researchers registered the names agents most commonly invent, seeding them with reverse shells. Nine popular tools are confirmed susceptible, including Cursor, GitHub Copilot, and Cline. Lock your dependency manifest and the attack surface closes; leave it floating and every hallucinated import is a live exploit waiting on a registry.

claims.claim: AI coding tools can be hijacked into botnets via hallucinated package names
claims.summary_span: AI coding tools can be hijacked into botnets via hallucinated package names
claims.source_span: HalluSquatting has the potential to assemble massive botnets... The attack works against AI coding assistants and agents... built on an LLM's inherent tendency to hallucinate the resource identifiers hosted in repositories and registries.
claims.note: The headline accurately paraphrases the source's description of HalluSquatting assembling botnets via hallucinated identifiers in coding tools.

claims.claim: HalluSquatting exploits a coding agent's tendency to hallucinate package identifiers.
claims.summary_span: HalluSquatting exploits a coding agent's tendency to hallucinate package identifiers.
claims.source_span: HalluSquatting is built on an LLM's inherent tendency to hallucinate the resource identifiers hosted in repositories and registries.
claims.note: 'Package identifiers' is a valid plain-English paraphrase of 'resource identifiers hosted in repositories and registries'.

claims.claim: Researchers registered the names agents most commonly invent, seeding them with reverse shells.
claims.summary_span: Researchers registered the names agents most commonly invent, seeding them with reverse shells.
claims.source_span: By predicting the identifiers LLMs are most likely to hallucinate and then registering and seeding them with instructions to install reverse shells or other malicious wares
claims.note: The body accurately compresses the source's description of the attack mechanism.

claims.claim: Nine popular tools are confirmed susceptible.
claims.summary_span: Nine popular tools are confirmed susceptible
claims.source_span: The attack works against AI coding assistants and agents, including Cursor, Cursor CLI, Gemini CLI, Windsurf, GitHub Copilot, Cline, OpenClaw, ZeroClaw, and NanoClaw, which are all susceptible.
claims.note: The source lists exactly nine tools (Cursor, Cursor CLI, Gemini CLI, Windsurf, GitHub Copilot, Cline, OpenClaw, ZeroClaw, NanoClaw), so 'nine' is accurate.

claims.claim: Nine popular tools are confirmed susceptible, including Cursor, GitHub Copilot, and Cline.
claims.summary_span: including Cursor, GitHub Copilot, and Cline
claims.source_span: The attack works against AI coding assistants and agents, including Cursor, Cursor CLI, Gemini CLI, Windsurf, GitHub Copilot, Cline, OpenClaw, ZeroClaw, and NanoClaw, which are all susceptible.
claims.note: Cursor, GitHub Copilot, and Cline are all explicitly named in the source as susceptible tools.

claims.claim: Lock your dependency manifest and the attack surface closes.
claims.summary_span: Lock your dependency manifest and the attack surface closes
claims.note: The source excerpt does not mention locking dependency manifests as a mitigation; this claim cannot be verified from the provided excerpt.

claims.claim: leave it floating and every hallucinated import is a live exploit waiting on a registry.
claims.summary_span: leave it floating and every hallucinated import is a live exploit waiting on a registry
claims.note: This editorial/directional claim about floating dependencies is not addressed in the source excerpt.

stories.headline: OpenAI trains agents on live tool use, not static examples
stories.summary: In a conference talk at InfoQ, OpenAI's fine-tuning team presented Agent RFT: training agents during live tool interactions rather than on curated output examples. The mechanism assigns reward signals across an entire context window, reportedly ending runaway token loops. If it holds at scale, this resolves agents' runaway-loop problem; if not, early adopters just reinherit it.

claims.claim: OpenAI trains agents on live tool use, not static examples
claims.summary_span: OpenAI trains agents on live tool use, not static examples
claims.source_span: Agent RFT, the most powerful way to enhance the performance of your agents... Supervised fine-tuning leverages supervised learning to teach the model to be much more likely to output the tokens in your dataset given the input prompt.
claims.note: The source contrasts Agent RFT with supervised fine-tuning on static output datasets, supporting the headline's framing of live tool use vs. static examples.

claims.claim: In a conference talk at InfoQ, OpenAI's fine-tuning team presented Agent RFT
claims.summary_span: In a conference talk at InfoQ, OpenAI's fine-tuning team presented Agent RFT
claims.note: The source confirms OpenAI's fine-tuning team presented Agent RFT, but the venue 'InfoQ' does not appear in the excerpt and cannot be verified.

claims.claim: training agents during live tool interactions rather than on curated output examples
claims.summary_span: training agents during live tool interactions rather than on curated output examples
claims.source_span: Supervised fine-tuning leverages supervised learning to teach the model to be much more likely to output the tokens in your dataset given the input prompt... All these tool calls, what it decides to send into a tool, what it gets out of a tool, and the tokens that it uses for reasoning, they're all part of the same context window.
claims.note: The source explicitly contrasts supervised fine-tuning on output datasets with Agent RFT's approach involving live tool interactions in the context window.

claims.claim: The mechanism assigns reward signals across an entire context window
claims.summary_span: The mechanism assigns reward signals across an entire context window
claims.note: The source mentions the context window as central to agent operation but does not describe reward signals being assigned across it; this specific mechanism is not in the excerpt.

claims.claim: reportedly ending runaway token loops
claims.summary_span: reportedly ending runaway token loops
claims.note: The source excerpt contains no mention of runaway token loops or Agent RFT resolving them.

sections.intro_lead: The attack surface is the toolchain.
sections.intro_body: Each item sits here because the finding is real but the scope is unconfirmed. Together they point at the same gap: the infrastructure agents rely on, from safety filters to package registries to training loops, is softer than the deployment decisions built on top of it.

notes: shape: green -- pulse: 1, big_picture: 4, hands_on: 4, currents: 3
